name: Newspeak
summary: Gilad Bracha's Smalltalk-family object-capability language. Top-level classes are stateless module *definitions*; instantiation accepts a `platform` parameter that is the sole source of external authority — module composition becomes capability passing. Active OSS Smalltalk-descendant; canonical example of an ocap-disciplined module system.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Newspeak** (Gilad Bracha, 2007+) is a Smalltalk-family object-capability language designed around the thesis that authority is conveyed *only* by holding object references. There is no global namespace, no static state, and no ambient ability to do I/O — every effect requires receiving a reference from a creator.

The defining design choice: top-level classes are stateless module *definitions*, not running modules. Instantiation accepts a `platform` parameter that is the sole source of external authority — module composition becomes capability passing. A class that needs file access must be passed a filesystem capability at construction time; it cannot acquire one through a global.

sections.title: Modules as capability passing
sections.content:
Newspeak's module system is the cleanest example of "the module *is* an object, instantiation *is* capability injection." A class definition has no ambient access to anything — even other classes in the same compilation unit are accessed via the enclosing class's reference graph, not via a top-level namespace lookup.

This aligns the module-system design with the ocap discipline: the only way to gain authority is to be handed a reference. The pattern generalises — Bracha's later "modules as objects" papers argue that top-level *static* state is the original sin of language-level module systems, and that treating modules as parameterised objects subsumes most of what import systems try to do via lexical resolution.

Newspeak is also distinctive for its **mirror-based reflection**: reflection is performed through a separate mirror object passed in as a capability, not via a global `Class.forName`-style API — so a Newspeak component can be instantiated without reflection authority simply by not passing it a mirror.

sections.title: Implementations and status
sections.content:
Implementations: the canonical Newspeak runs on a Smalltalk-style VM (originally Squeak/Pharo derivatives, then dedicated Newspeak VMs). The language has had a consistent implementation since 2008 and is open source.

Status (as of 2026-04): active OSS niche language. Newspeak's primary influence in 2026 is conceptual: its module-as-object discipline shows up in WASI Component Model `world`s (modules as parameterised by imports), in Joe Duffy's Midori "objects-as-capabilities" framing, and in the SES/Hardened JS `Compartment` model. Bracha's continuing essays argue these are independent rediscoveries of the Newspeak module discipline.
